requirements.summary: The character data in a UTF-8 Encoded String MUST be well-formed UTF-8 as defined by the Unicode specification and restated in RFC 3629 . In particular, the character data MUST NOT include encodings of code points between U+D800 and U+DFFF .

evidence.reason: validate_mqtt_string uses std::str::from_utf8() to reject ill-formed UTF-8 including surrogates (U+D800-U+DFFF). read_mqtt_string delegates to it, and the v5 write_mqtt_string wrapper validates before serializing.
Publish.topic remains Bytes, but Publish::read validates the topic bytes immediately after read_mqtt_bytes and Publish::write validates them before write_mqtt_bytes.
LastWill.topic remains Bytes, but LastWill::read validates the will topic bytes immediately after read_mqtt_bytes and LastWill::write validates them before write_mqtt_bytes.
Runtime receiver handling maps TopicNotUtf8 decode errors to a Malformed Packet DISCONNECT reason.

requirements.summary: A UTF-8 Encoded String MUST NOT include an encoding of the null character U+0000. . If a receiver (Server or Client) receives an MQTT Control Packet containing U+0000 it is a Malformed Packet. Refer to section 4.13 for information about handling errors.
requirements.mapping_reason: validate_mqtt_string rejects U+0000 after well-formed UTF-8 validation. read_mqtt_string delegates to it, the v5 write_mqtt_string wrapper validates all outgoing UTF-8 string fields, and Bytes-backed Publish.topic and LastWill.topic validate on both read and write.

evidence.reason: validate_mqtt_string rejects literal U+0000 bytes with Error::MalformedPacket. read_mqtt_string delegates to it, and the v5 write_mqtt_string wrapper validates before serializing.
Publish.topic is validated after read_mqtt_bytes and before write_mqtt_bytes, so Bytes-backed topic storage cannot bypass the U+0000 check.
client_id and other String-backed fields use read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string validation. LastWill.topic is validated on both read and write.

requirements.summary: A UTF-8 encoded sequence 0xEF 0xBB 0xBF is always interpreted as U+FEFF ("ZERO WIDTH NO-BREAK SPACE") wherever it appears in a string and MUST NOT be skipped over or stripped off by a packet receiver .
requirements.mapping_reason: read_mqtt_string uses std::str::from_utf8() followed by to_owned(), which preserves the BOM (0xEF 0xBB 0xBF = U+FEFF) as a regular character. There is no stripping or skipping logic anywhere in the decode path. write_mqtt_string writes the string bytes as-is via write_mqtt_bytes, preserving the BOM. The implementation naturally satisfies this requirement by never manipulating decoded string content.

evidence.reason: read_mqtt_string calls from_utf8() then to_owned() with no filtering or stripping. The BOM sequence 0xEF 0xBB 0xBF decodes to U+FEFF and is preserved. write_mqtt_string delegates to write_mqtt_bytes which writes raw bytes without modification.
v5 module re-exports core primitives directly with no wrapping or post-processing that could strip the BOM.

test_coverage.notes: A targeted test verifies that read_mqtt_string preserves a leading U+FEFF character.

requirements.summary: The Variable Byte Integer is encoded using an encoding scheme which uses a single byte for values up to 127. Larger values are handled as follows. The encoding uses 7 data bits per byte with bit 7 as the continuation flag, limits to 4 bytes max, and caps at 268435455. v5 also uses the same VBI decoder for property lengths via the length() wrapper.

requirements.summary: Both strings MUST comply with the requirements for UTF-8 Encoded Strings . If a receiver (Client or Server) receives a string pair which does not meet these requirements it is a Malformed Packet. Refer to section 4.13 for information about handling errors.

requirements.summary: The remaining bits of byte 1 in the Fixed Header contain flags specific to each MQTT Control Packet type as shown below. Where a flag bit is marked as “Reserved”, it is reserved for future use and MUST be set to the value listed .
requirements.mapping_reason: The v5 Packet::read path now validates reserved flag bits in the fixed header via validate_fixed_header_flags(), ported from v4 with the addition of the Auth packet type. The function checks: flags==0 for most types, flags==0b0010 for SUBSCRIBE/UNSUBSCRIBE/PUBREL, and any flags for PUBLISH. IncorrectPacketFormat is returned internally on violation. InboundDisconnect::classify maps IncorrectPacketFormat to DisconnectReasonCode::MalformedPacket because MQTT v5 section 2.1.3 classifies invalid fixed-header flags as a Malformed Packet. Disconnect::read also preserves standalone public-parser validation for packet type and flags. The write side was already compliant (all hardcoded first bytes use correct flag values).

Disconnect::read validates packet type and reserved fixed-header flags itself, preserving correctness for direct callers of this public parser. Invalid DISCONNECT flags return IncorrectPacketFormat.

IncorrectPacketFormat is mapped to DisconnectReasonCode::MalformedPacket. MQTT v5 section 2.1.3 states that invalid fixed-header flags are a Malformed Packet; section 4.13 then uses reason code 0x81 for Malformed Packet.

requirements.summary: A PUBLISH packet MUST NOT contain a Packet Identifier if its QoS value is set to 0 .

Length calculation: Publish::len() excludes the 2-byte pkid field for QoS 0. A QoS 0 Publish struct with a non-zero pkid has its pkid silently ignored at encode time rather than explicitly rejected, but the wire format is still correct.

requirements.summary: After a Network Connection is established by a Client to a Server, the first packet sent from the Client to the Server MUST be a CONNECT packet .
requirements.mapping_reason: The v5 eventloop architecturally guarantees CONNECT is the first packet sent on every new network connection. When self.network is None, poll() calls connect() which first establishes the TCP/TLS/WS transport via network_connect() and then immediately sends CONNECT via mqtt_connect() before returning. No other packet can be sent before CONNECT because: (1) the network object does not exist until connect() returns, (2) the Request enum has no Connect variant so users cannot inject a CONNECT through request channels, and (3) the select() method (which processes user requests) only runs after self.network = Some(network). The eventloop also validates that the first server response is CONNACK or AUTH, returning ConnectionError::NotConnAck otherwise.

evidence.reason: poll() calls connect() when network is None. connect() calls network_connect() then mqtt_connect(), which sends CONNECT as the very first MQTT packet on the new transport. No other packet path can execute before self.network is set.

mqtt_connect_inner() constructs a Connect packet and writes it to the network, then awaits a ConnAck or AUTH response. ConnectionError::NotConnAck is returned if the first response is not ConnAck or AUTH.
The Request enum has no Connect variant, so users cannot submit a CONNECT packet through the request channel. CONNECT is only sent internally by mqtt_connect_inner().

test_coverage.notes: Wire-level test verifies the first packet sent by mqtt_connect is CONNECT. Eventloop-level test queues a request before connection establishment and verifies CONNECT is sent before the queued request on the same socket.

requirements.summary: A Client can only send the CONNECT packet once over a Network Connection. The Server MUST process a second CONNECT packet sent from a Client as a Protocol Error and close the Network Connection . Refer to section 4.13 for information about handling errors.
requirements.mapping_reason: The client cannot send a second CONNECT on the same network connection. The Request enum has no Connect variant, so users cannot submit a CONNECT through request channels. The CONNECT packet is only sent internally by mqtt_connect_inner(), which is called exactly once per connection attempt inside connect(), which itself only runs when self.network is None. After a successful connection, self.network = Some(network) and subsequent poll() calls go to select(), which never calls mqtt_connect() again. When the connection is lost, self.clean() sets self.network = None, and the next poll() call re-establishes a new network connection (new TCP socket) before sending CONNECT again. The server-side obligation (MUST close on second CONNECT) is outside client scope.

evidence.reason: The Request enum has no Connect variant (15 variants: Publish, PubAck, PubRec, PubComp, PubRel, PingReq, PingResp, Subscribe, SubAck, Unsubscribe, UnsubAck, Auth, Disconnect, DisconnectNow, DisconnectWithTimeout), making it impossible for users to submit a CONNECT packet through the request channel.

mqtt_connect_inner() is the function that writes the CONNECT packet, and it is reached only through mqtt_connect() during connect(). connect() only runs from poll() when self.network is None. After connect() succeeds, self.network is set and mqtt_connect_inner() is never called again on the same connection.
When the connection is lost, clean() sets self.network = None, causing the next poll() to establish a completely new network connection before sending CONNECT again.

test_coverage.notes: Variant-enumeration test documents the current Request surface while the Request enum definition provides the compile-time evidence that no Connect variant exists. Eventloop-level wire test verifies that, after CONNECT succeeds, a later queued request on the same socket is sent as that request rather than as a second CONNECT.

requirements.summary: If the User Name Flag is set to 0, a User Name MUST NOT be present in the Payload . If the User Name Flag is set to 1, a User Name MUST be present in the Payload .
requirements.mapping_reason: This requirement combines two clauses: (1) username_flag=0 → username MUST NOT be present, and (2) username_flag=1 → username MUST be present. Encode path: ConnectAuth::None writes zero username bytes and returns flags=0 (no username flag), so no username appears in the payload. ConnectAuth::Username and ConnectAuth::UsernamePassword both write a length-prefixed username string and set the 0x80 flag bit. The Rust type system guarantees that when auth is Username or UsernamePassword, a username String is always present. Decode path: ConnectAuth::read() returns ConnectAuth::None without reading any username bytes when username_flag=false. When username_flag=true, read_mqtt_string() is always called to consume a username; truncation produces an error. The trailing-data check (bytes.has_remaining()) in Connect::read() rejects any extra payload bytes beyond what the flags indicate, which catches a username present when the flag is 0. Note: unlike v3.1.1, v5 allows password-without-username (ConnectAuth::Password variant), so validate_connect_flags() does not reject that combination.

evidence.reason: Encode: ConnectAuth::None writes 0 bytes and returns flags=0. ConnectAuth::Username writes the username string and returns 0x80. ConnectAuth::UsernamePassword writes both and returns 0xC0. ConnectAuth::Password writes only password bytes and returns 0x40 (no username flag, no username bytes).

requirements.summary: The Keep Alive is a Two Byte Integer which is a time interval measured in seconds. It is the maximum time interval that is permitted to elapse between the point at which the Client finishes transmitting one MQTT Control Packet and the point it starts sending the next.
requirements.mapping_reason: The Connect struct stores keep_alive as u16, encoded via buffer.put_u16() and decoded via read_u16(). MqttOptions::set_keep_alive accepts u16 seconds, converted to Duration internally. The eventloop uses the Duration to schedule periodic PINGREQ packets at the keep-alive interval while the connection is open. This is conservative for the client's outbound packet deadline, though the timer is not reset after every ordinary outbound packet. The Two Byte Integer (u16) representation is enforced by the type system and wire encoding. Equivalent to v4's MQTT-3.1.2-23.

evidence.reason: keep_alive is u16, written with put_u16 and read with read_u16, ensuring Two Byte Integer wire representation.

Eventloop schedules periodic PINGREQ packets when the keep_alive Duration elapses. mqtt_connect_inner converts Duration back to u16 for the CONNECT packet.

requirements.summary: If the Keep Alive value is non-zero and the Server does not receive an MQTT Control Packet from the Client within one and a half times the Keep Alive time period, it MUST close the Network Connection to the Client as if the network had failed .

The client has no obligation to enforce this timeout; it is purely a server-side behavior. The client does implement the complementary behavior of sending PINGREQ within the keep-alive interval to prevent the server from triggering this timeout, but that is covered by MQTT-3.1.2-20. requirements.summary: The Client and Server MUST store the Session State after the Network Connection is closed if the Session Expiry Interval is greater than 0 .
requirements.mapping_reason: The client can request a non-zero Session Expiry Interval through MqttOptions::set_session_expiry_interval(), which is serialized into CONNECT properties by ConnectProperties::write(). On reconnect, EventLoop::reconcile_connack_session() preserves local pending/session state when clean_start=false and the broker reports session_present=true; it resets local state when the broker reports session_present=false or when clean_start=true. The EventLoop also tracks the ClientID associated with retained local state and clears that state before reconnecting with a different ClientID, preventing session state from one MQTT identity from being reused under another.

evidence.reason: CONNECT properties include session_expiry_interval: Option<u32>. The codec reads PropertyType::SessionExpiryInterval via read_u32 and writes it via put_u32, allowing the client to request a non-zero Session Expiry Interval.
MqttOptions exposes the configured Session Expiry Interval and stores it in CONNECT properties used by mqtt_connect_inner().

reconcile_connack_session() preserves pending local state when clean_start=false and session_present=true, and resets state when the broker reports no resumed session. reset_session_state_if_client_id_changed() prevents retained local state from crossing ClientID boundaries. On connection loss, EventLoop::clean() moves unacknowledged outgoing state into the pending queue for replay on a resumed session.
MqttState holds client-side session state, including outgoing QoS state that can be cleaned into pending requests for reconnect/replay when the session is resumed.

test_coverage.notes: Unit tests cover Session Expiry option plumbing and CONNACK session reconciliation. Reliability tests configure clean_start=false with a non-zero Session Expiry Interval, then verify pending QoS state is retained/replayed when the broker resumes the session. ClientID guard tests cover the identity boundary for retained local state.

requirements.summary: The Payload of the CONNECT packet contains one or more length-prefixed fields, whose presence is determined by the flags in the Variable Header. These fields, if present, MUST appear in the order Client Identifier, Will Properties, Will Topic, Will Payload, User Name, Password .

requirements.summary: If a Server receives a CONNECT packet containing a Will QoS that exceeds its capabilities, it MUST reject the connection. It SHOULD use a CONNACK packet with Reason Code 0x9B (QoS not supported) as described in section 4.13 Handling errors, and MUST close the Network Connection .
requirements.mapping_reason: This requirement obligates the Server to reject a CONNECT with a Will QoS exceeding its capabilities, close the Network Connection, and SHOULD use CONNACK reason code 0x9B. The client's responsibility is limited to correctly encoding the Will QoS in the CONNECT packet (covered by MQTT-3.1.2-12) and being able to decode a CONNACK reason code if the server sends one. The v5 client can decode ConnectReturnCode::QoSNotSupported (0x9B/155) from a CONNACK response, but the obligation to reject and close is entirely server-side.

evidence.reason: The client can decode CONNACK reason code 0x9B (155) as ConnectReturnCode::QoSNotSupported. The server-side behavior is to reject and close; using this specific reason code is recommended by SHOULD.
The client correctly encodes and decodes Will QoS in the CONNECT packet (covered by MQTT-3.1.2-12). Will QoS=3 is rejected as Malformed Packet; valid Will QoS values 0-2 are correctly serialized.
